article_title: I thought Logseq was too simple but ended up using it for everything that mattered
core_thesis: The article argues that the most effective productivity tool is not the one with the most features, but the one you trust and will actually use consistently. The author describes how Logseq, a free, open-source, local-first note-taking and task management app built around daily journaling and linked pages, won her over precisely because of its simplicity and predictability, in contrast to more complex tools like Notion and Obsidian that she abandoned due to feature overload and lack of trust.
detailed_summary: The author recounts her history of jumping between productivity apps—Notion, Obsidian, and various task managers—each of which she would set up elaborately but eventually abandon. She found that these 'smarter' apps introduced mental friction: Notion's relational databases behaved unpredictably, Obsidian plugins conflicted or broke, and task managers applied forgotten rules. This eroded her trust and made her hesitant to rely on them. Logseq, by contrast, offers a blank daily journal page where you write bullets and link pages with double brackets. Nothing moves or re-categorizes itself; the app simply stores what you give it and retrieves it faithfully. This predictability built trust, which the author argues is more important than any feature set. She describes how Logseq's daily page format encouraged her to just write and link, automatically building a graph of connected ideas. For client check-ins, project notes, and weekly planning, she writes on today's page, tags relevant pages, and Logseq threads everything into a running history viewable on each page. The local-first, plain Markdown storage gives her true ownership of her data, eliminating concerns about vendor lock-in or subscription costs. Ultimately, the author concludes that the right productivity system is the one you'll open tomorrow, and Logseq's quiet reliability made it that system for her.

entities.name: Logseq
entities.role: Free, open-source, local-first note-taking and task management app that is the subject of the article.

entities.name: Notion
entities.role: A feature-rich productivity app the author previously used and abandoned due to complexity and unpredictability.

entities.name: Obsidian
entities.role: A note-taking app with plugins that the author abandoned due to plugin conflicts and breakage.

entities.name: Beatrice Manuel
entities.role: Author of the article, a productivity expert with a background in Commerce, Accounting, and Insurance.

key_data_points: Logseq is a free, open-source, local-first note-taking and task management app built around a daily journal and a graph of linked pages (🟢 relevant to your productivity: it's a low-friction tool that can help you organize study notes, project tasks, and research without overhead).

The author abandoned Notion because its relational databases cascaded changes unpredictably, Obsidian because plugins conflicted or broke, and task managers because they applied forgotten rules (🟢 relevant to your student needs: complex tools can waste time on maintenance instead of actual work).

Logseq opens to a blank daily page; you write bullets and link to other pages using double brackets. Nothing moves or re-categorizes itself, providing predictability and trust (🟢 relevant to your programming mindset: the tool is deterministic and transparent, like a well-written function).

Logseq stores everything as plain Markdown files on your device, giving you full ownership and eliminating subscription costs or vendor lock-in (🟢 relevant to your homelab/devops interests: aligns with self-hosting and data sovereignty principles).

The author uses Logseq for client check-ins, project notes, and weekly planning by writing on the daily page and tagging relevant pages; backlinks automatically thread everything into a running history (🟢 relevant to your student life: can be used to track assignments, lecture notes, and research topics with minimal setup).
methodology_steps: Open Logseq to the daily journal page (it opens automatically).
Write your thoughts, tasks, or notes as bullet points. Use double brackets [[Page Name]] to link to other pages; Logseq will create the page if it doesn't exist.
For recurring topics (e.g., a client, a project, a class), tag the relevant page in each daily entry. Logseq will automatically collect all linked bullets on that page's backlinks section.
For weekly or monthly planning, create a single page entry (e.g., 'Week 10 Planning') and inter-link it to project pages and task pages. No separate tool needed.
To review a topic, open its page and see all linked entries from your daily journals in chronological order. No manual filing required.
conclusions: The most important feature of a productivity tool is trust, not feature count. Logseq's simplicity and predictability built trust, making the author more consistent in using it (🟢 relevant to your student productivity: a tool you trust is one you'll actually use daily).

Local-first, plain Markdown storage gives users true ownership of their data, eliminating concerns about vendor lock-in or subscription costs, which encourages long-term commitment (🟢 relevant to your homelab/devops: aligns with self-hosting and data control principles).

Daily journaling with backlinks is a low-friction organization method that automatically builds a searchable history without requiring upfront categorization or folder maintenance (🟢 relevant to your programming and research: can be used to log code snippets, debugging notes, and project ideas with automatic cross-referencing).
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to you as a student and programmer because it presents a productivity tool that minimizes overhead and maximizes trust. Logseq's daily journal and backlink system can help you organize lecture notes, project tasks, and research without the complexity of Notion or Obsidian. Its local-first, plain Markdown storage aligns with your homelab/devops interests in self-hosting and data control. For your red teaming work, you could use Logseq to log attack paths, tool configurations, and findings with automatic cross-referencing. The article's core lesson—that a simple, predictable tool you trust beats a feature-rich one you abandon—is a valuable mindset for choosing any tool in your workflow.
extra_advice: Consider using Logseq as a central knowledge base for your cybersecurity studies and red teaming projects. Create pages for each vulnerability class (e.g., [[SQL Injection]], [[XSS]]), each tool (e.g., [[Burp Suite]], [[Metasploit]]), and each engagement. Daily journal entries can log your progress, and backlinks will automatically build a searchable history. You can also integrate Logseq with Git for version control of your Markdown files, giving you a self-hosted, auditable research log.
